segments.title: A blockade force at Montevideo
segments.summary: Force K's cruiser force should have been sufficient to contain Admiral Graf Spee if the task was merely a blockade off Montevideo.
segments.body: Clarke begins with a force-composition problem: why rush Force K toward Montevideo if the Royal Navy intended only to blockade Admiral Graf Spee? HMS Cumberland had joined the damaged cruisers Achilles and Ajax, while the German raider had also been battered in the River Plate action. He judges that combination probably sufficient to maintain a heavy blockade. The comparison matters because a force already suited to containment makes the arrival of still more powerful ships evidence of a more demanding contingency.

evidence.note: Clarke weighs Cumberland, Achilles, and Ajax against the needs of a simple blockade.

segments.title: Renown closes the escape route
segments.summary: HMS Renown made a breakout by Admiral Graf Spee far less plausible, turning a blockade into a secure trap.
segments.body: Clarke next tests the possibility that Admiral Graf Spee might fight through the cruiser cordon. HMS Renown supplied the answer to that danger by making a successful breakout far less plausible. Her presence turned the deployment from a probable blockade into a trap backed by a fast battlecruiser. This watch point shows how the Royal Navy matched a specific reinforcement to the raider's remaining escape option rather than merely adding another hull to the station.

evidence.note: Clarke says Renown would ensure the raider could not find a way out.

segments.title: Ark Royal and the Montevideo contingency
segments.summary: Sending Ark Royal and the newly reconstructed Renown suggested that Force K was preparing for a showdown beyond an ordinary blockade.
segments.body: Clarke stresses that Ark Royal was Britain's newest strike carrier and that Renown had emerged from a reconstruction so extensive that calling it a refit understates the work. Sending both high-value ships for routine containment therefore does not fit his reading of Royal Navy behaviour. He argues that Force K was preparing for Uruguay to prove unable to enforce its will on Admiral Graf Spee, or for the raider to attempt an escape toward Argentina. Ark Royal's aircraft would then give the British a way to finish the operation whether the target was at sea or still in harbour. That possibility moves the issue beyond blockade tactics into neutrality law and naval diplomacy. Clarke explicitly acknowledges that an attack in harbour would break the law, while presenting the deployment as evidence that operational success could take priority over that constraint.

evidence.note: Clarke identifies Ark Royal as the newest strike carrier and Renown as recently reconstructed.
He links the force to a possible failure of Uruguayan control and an attack in harbour or at sea.
